(born August 31, 1973) is a Canadian former [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") [defenceman](/wiki/Defenceman "Defenceman") and current special assignment coach of the [Anaheim Ducks](/wiki/Anaheim_Ducks "Anaheim Ducks"). He played 18 seasons and over [1,000 games](/wiki/List_of_NHL_players_with_1000_games_played "List of NHL players with 1000 games played") in the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") (NHL) for the [New Jersey Devils](/wiki/New_Jersey_Devils "New Jersey Devils") and Anaheim Ducks. Niedermayer is a four-time [Stanley Cup](/wiki/Stanley_Cup "Stanley Cup") champion and played in five [NHL All-Star Games](/wiki/NHL_All-Star_Game "NHL All-Star Game"). He won the [James Norris Memorial Trophy](/wiki/James_Norris_Memorial_Trophy "James Norris Memorial Trophy") in [2003–04](/wiki/2003%E2%80%9304_NHL_season "2003–04 NHL season") as the NHL's top defenceman and the [Conn Smythe Trophy](/wiki/Conn_Smythe_Trophy "Conn Smythe Trophy") in [2007](/wiki/2007_Stanley_Cup_playoffs "2007 Stanley Cup playoffs") as the most valuable player of the playoffs. In 2017, Niedermayer was named one of the [100 Greatest NHL Players](/wiki/100_Greatest_NHL_Players "100 Greatest NHL Players") in history.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

**Scott Niedermayer** (born August 31, 1973) is a Canadian former [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") [defenceman](/wiki/Defenceman "Defenceman") and current special assignment coach of the [Anaheim Ducks](/wiki/Anaheim_Ducks "Anaheim Ducks"). He played 18 seasons and over [1,000 games](/wiki/List_of_NHL_players_with_1000_games_played "List of NHL players with 1000 games played") in the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") (NHL) for the [New Jersey Devils](/wiki/New_Jersey_Devils "New Jersey Devils") and Anaheim Ducks. Niedermayer is a four-time [Stanley Cup](/wiki/Stanley_Cup "Stanley Cup") champion and played in five [NHL All-Star Games](/wiki/NHL_All-Star_Game "NHL All-Star Game"). He won the [James Norris Memorial Trophy](/wiki/James_Norris_Memorial_Trophy "James Norris Memorial Trophy") in [2003–04](/wiki/2003%E2%80%9304_NHL_season "2003–04 NHL season") as the NHL's top defenceman and the [Conn Smythe Trophy](/wiki/Conn_Smythe_Trophy "Conn Smythe Trophy") in [2007](/wiki/2007_Stanley_Cup_playoffs "2007 Stanley Cup playoffs") as the most valuable player of the playoffs. In 2017, Niedermayer was named one of the [100 Greatest NHL Players](/wiki/100_Greatest_NHL_Players "100 Greatest NHL Players") in history.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

As a [junior](/wiki/Junior_hockey "Junior hockey"), Niedermayer was a member of a [Kamloops Blazers](/wiki/Kamloops_Blazers "Kamloops Blazers") team that won two [Western Hockey League championships](/wiki/Ed_Chynoweth_Cup "Ed Chynoweth Cup") and was voted the [most valuable player](/wiki/Stafford_Smythe_Memorial_Trophy "Stafford Smythe Memorial Trophy") of the [1992 Memorial Cup](/wiki/1992_Memorial_Cup "1992 Memorial Cup"), leading the Blazers to the [Canadian Hockey League](/wiki/Canadian_Hockey_League "Canadian Hockey League") championship. The third overall selection at the [1991 NHL entry draft](/wiki/1991_NHL_entry_draft "1991 NHL entry draft") by New Jersey, Niedermayer played the majority of his professional career with the Devils before moving to Anaheim in 2005.

Internationally, Niedermayer played with [Team Canada](/wiki/Canada_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Canada men's national ice hockey team") on several occasions. He is a member of the [Triple Gold Club](/wiki/Triple_Gold_Club "Triple Gold Club"), having won the Stanley Cup as well as a [World Championship](/wiki/Ice_Hockey_World_Championships "Ice Hockey World Championships") ([2004](/wiki/2004_Men%27s_World_Ice_Hockey_Championships "2004 Men's World Ice Hockey Championships")) and [Olympic](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games "Ice hockey at the Olympic Games") gold medals ([2002](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2002_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 2002 Winter Olympics"), [2010](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2010_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 2010 Winter Olympics")). Niedermayer also played for the [Memorial Cup](/wiki/Memorial_Cup "Memorial Cup") champions, and championship teams at the [1991 World Junior Championships](/wiki/1991_World_Junior_Ice_Hockey_Championships "1991 World Junior Ice Hockey Championships") and the [2004 World Cup of Hockey](/wiki/2004_World_Cup_of_Hockey "2004 World Cup of Hockey"), making him and [Corey Perry](/wiki/Corey_Perry "Corey Perry") the only players in history to have ever won each of the six major North American and international competitions available to players. He was introduced in to the [IIHF All-Time Canada Team](/wiki/IIHF_All-Time_Teams "IIHF All-Time Teams") in 2020.

Regarded as one of the greatest defencemen in NHL history, Niedermayer has earned numerous accolades throughout his career. He was inducted into [Canada's Sports Hall of Fame](/wiki/Canada%27s_Sports_Hall_of_Fame "Canada's Sports Hall of Fame") in 2012, into the [Hockey Hall of Fame](/wiki/Hockey_Hall_of_Fame "Hockey Hall of Fame") in November 2013, and into the [IIHF Hall of Fame](/wiki/IIHF_Hall_of_Fame "IIHF Hall of Fame") in 2015. The New Jersey Devils, Anaheim Ducks, and Kamloops Blazers have all [retired](/wiki/List_of_NHL_retired_numbers "List of NHL retired numbers") his uniform number.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scott_Niedermayer&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Niedermayer was born in [Edmonton](/wiki/Edmonton "Edmonton"), [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta"), but spent the first three years of his life in [Cassiar, British Columbia](/wiki/Cassiar,_British_Columbia "Cassiar, British Columbia") before his family settled in [Cranbrook, British Columbia](/wiki/Cranbrook,_British_Columbia "Cranbrook, British Columbia").[[3]](#cite_note-straight2010-3) His father, Bob, was a doctor in Cassiar and then Cranbrook, and his mother Carol was a teacher.[[4]](#cite_note-SI1996Profile-4) He has a younger brother, [Rob](/wiki/Rob_Niedermayer "Rob Niedermayer").[[5]](#cite_note-McEvoy-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Scott and his brother were inseparable when they were younger and often played hockey together. While their father was often their team doctor, their mother taught them to skate. She enrolled them in [figure skating](/wiki/Figure_skating "Figure skating") to aid their skills development and taught power skating classes in Cranbrook in exchange for ice time for her sons.[[4]](#cite_note-SI1996Profile-4) An offensive [defenceman](/wiki/Defenceman "Defenceman"), Scott led his Cranbrook [midget](/wiki/Minor_hockey "Minor hockey") team in scoring with 55 goals and 92 points in 1988–89.[[7]](#cite_note-NHLRetire-7)

## Playing career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scott_Niedermayer&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Playing career")]

### Junior

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scott_Niedermayer&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Junior")]

Niedermayer played three seasons of [junior hockey](/wiki/Junior_hockey "Junior hockey") with the [Kamloops Blazers](/wiki/Kamloops_Blazers "Kamloops Blazers") of the [Western Hockey League](/wiki/Western_Hockey_League "Western Hockey League") (WHL) between 1989 and 1992.[[8]](#cite_note-BlazersRetired-8) He recorded 69 points in 64 games in his first season, [1989–90](/wiki/1989%E2%80%9390_WHL_season "1989–90 WHL season"),[[9]](#cite_note-NHLStats-9) and helped the Blazers win the [President's Cup](/wiki/Ed_Chynoweth_Cup "Ed Chynoweth Cup") as WHL champions.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The Blazers advanced to the [1990 Memorial Cup](/wiki/1990_Memorial_Cup "1990 Memorial Cup") as the top ranked team in Canada, but disappointed in the tournament by losing all three games.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Niedermayer earned several accolades in [1990–91](/wiki/1990%E2%80%9391_WHL_season "1990–91 WHL season"). He scored 26 goals and 82 points in 57 games to earn a place on the Western Conference All-Star team.[[12]](#cite_note-LOH-12) Additionally, he was named the recipient of the [Daryl K. (Doc) Seaman Trophy](/wiki/Daryl_K._(Doc)_Seaman_Trophy "Daryl K. (Doc) Seaman Trophy") as the WHL's scholastic player of the year and won the [Canadian Hockey League Scholastic Player of the Year](/wiki/CHL_Scholastic_Player_of_the_Year "CHL Scholastic Player of the Year") award.[[13]](#cite_note-ScholasticPOY-13)

A top prospect for the [1991 NHL entry draft](/wiki/1991_NHL_entry_draft "1991 NHL entry draft"), Niedermayer was selected in the first round, third overall, by the [New Jersey Devils](/wiki/New_Jersey_Devils "New Jersey Devils").[[14]](#cite_note-14) He began the [1991–92 season](/wiki/1991%E2%80%9392_NHL_season "1991–92 NHL season") with New Jersey as the team wanted him to experience the NHL before being returned to Kamloops. After sitting out the Devils first five games, Niedermayer made his NHL debut on October 16, 1991, against the [New York Rangers](/wiki/New_York_Rangers "New York Rangers").[[15]](#cite_note-15) He appeared in four games with the Devils, recording one assist, before he was sent back to junior.[[16]](#cite_note-BestPick3-16) Though he appeared in only 35 games in the [1991–92 WHL season](/wiki/1991%E2%80%9392_WHL_season "1991–92 WHL season"), Niedermayer's 39 points were enough to earn him a second berth on the West All-Star team.[[12]](#cite_note-LOH-12) After losing in the Western Conference Final the previous season, the Blazers rebounded to win their second WHL championship in three years in 1992.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Niedermayer tied for third place in playoff scoring with 23 points.[[18]](#cite_note-18) At the [1992 Memorial Cup](/wiki/1992_Memorial_Cup "1992 Memorial Cup"), he scored seven points in five games to lead the Blazers to the national championship.[[19]](#cite_note-19) He was voted the recipient of the [Stafford Smythe Memorial Trophy](/wiki/Stafford_Smythe_Memorial_Trophy "Stafford Smythe Memorial Trophy") as the most valuable player of the Memorial Cup.[[20]](#cite_note-SSMT-20)

### New Jersey Devils

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scott_Niedermayer&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: New Jersey Devils")]

Niedermayer joined the Devils full-time in [1992–93](/wiki/1992%E2%80%9393_NHL_season "1992–93 NHL season"). He scored his first NHL goal on November 8, 1992, against goaltender [Brian Hayward](/wiki/Brian_Hayward "Brian Hayward") in a 6–1 victory over the [San Jose Sharks](/wiki/San_Jose_Sharks "San Jose Sharks").[[21]](#cite_note-21) Overall, Niedermayer appeared in 80 games, scoring 11 goals and 40 points and was named to the [NHL All-Rookie Team](/wiki/NHL_All-Rookie_Team "NHL All-Rookie Team") on defence.[[9]](#cite_note-NHLStats-9)[[12]](#cite_note-LOH-12) Niedermayer improved to 48 points in [1993–94](/wiki/1993%E2%80%9394_NHL_season "1993–94 NHL season"), and added 4 points in 20 playoff games as the Devils reached the [Eastern Conference](/wiki/Eastern_Conference_(NHL) "Eastern Conference (NHL)") Final against the [New York Rangers](/wiki/New_York_Rangers "New York Rangers"), a series they lost in seven games.[[9]](#cite_note-NHLStats-9)[[22]](#cite_note-22) The Devils made another long playoff run in the [lockout](/wiki/1994%E2%80%9395_NHL_lockout "1994–95 NHL lockout")-shortened [1994–95 season](/wiki/1994%E2%80%9395_NHL_season "1994–95 NHL season"), reaching the [Stanley Cup Finals](/wiki/1995_Stanley_Cup_Finals "1995 Stanley Cup Finals") for the first time in franchise history.[[23]](#cite_note-1995SCC-23) Niedermayer scored 11 points in 20 playoff games,[[9]](#cite_note-NHLStats-9) including a key goal in game two of the Final as the Devils won the [Stanley Cup](/wiki/Stanley_Cup "Stanley Cup") with a four-game sweep of the [Detroit Red Wings](/wiki/Detroit_Red_Wings "Detroit Red Wings").[[23]](#cite_note-1995SCC-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

A 33-point season followed for Niedermayer in [1995–96](/wiki/1995%E2%80%9396_NHL_season "1995–96 NHL season"),[[9]](#cite_note-NHLStats-9) but the Devils became the first defending champion in 26 years to miss the playoffs.[[25]](#cite_note-25) After a 35-point season in [1996–97](/wiki/1996%E2%80%9397_NHL_season "1996–97 NHL season"), Niedermayer's best statistical season in New Jersey came in [1997–98](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_NHL_season "1997–98 NHL season") with a 14-goal, 57-point campaign.[[9]](#cite_note-NHLStats-9) He played in his first [NHL All-Star Game](/wiki/NHL_All-Star_Game "NHL All-Star Game"), scoring a goal,[[26]](#cite_note-26) and was named to the NHL's [Second All-Star team](/wiki/NHL_All-Star_team "NHL All-Star team") at the season's end.[[12]](#cite_note-LOH-12) After finishing second in league scoring amongst defencemen, despite playing in New Jersey's stifling [defensive system](/wiki/Neutral_zone_trap "Neutral zone trap") that suppresses offence,[[27]](#cite_note-SignUtah-27) Niedermayer demanded a significant raise. He rejected an offer that would have paid him a base salary of $3.25 million and, unable to come to terms before the start of the [1998–99 season](/wiki/1998%E2%80%9399_NHL_season "1998–99 NHL season"), began the year as a holdout.[[28]](#cite_note-FeelSorry-28) As the dispute dragged into the season, he joined the [Utah Grizzlies](/wiki/Utah_Grizzlies "Utah Grizzlies") of the [International Hockey League](/wiki/International_Hockey_League_(1945%E2%80%932001) "International Hockey League (1945–2001)") (IHL).[[27]](#cite_note-SignUtah-27) After missing the first month of the NHL season, Niedermayer and the Devils finally agreed to a multi-year contract, the terms of which were not released.[[29]](#cite_note-29) He appeared in 71 games with the Devils that season, recording 46 points.[[9]](#cite_note-NHLStats-9)

Late in the [1999–2000 season](/wiki/1999%E2%80%932000_NHL_season "1999–2000 NHL season"), Niedermayer was involved in a violent incident with [Peter Worell](/wiki/Peter_Worell "Peter Worell") of the [Florida Panthers](/wiki/Florida_Panthers "Florida Panthers"). After being [elbowed](/wiki/Elbowing "Elbowing") by Worell, Niedermayer responded by swinging his stick at his opponent's head. Worell suffered a concussion and missed six games, while Niedermayer was suspended for ten games – nine in the regular season, and New Jersey's first playoff game.[[30]](#cite_note-30) After returning from his ban, Niedermayer's steady defensive contributions in the playoffs helped the Devils win their second Stanley Cup by defeating the [Dallas Stars](/wiki/Dallas_Stars "Dallas Stars") in six games in the [2000 Stanley Cup Finals](/wiki/2000_Stanley_Cup_Finals "2000 Stanley Cup Finals").[[31]](#cite_note-31) While celebrating his day with the Cup, Niedermayer took the trophy to Fisher Peak, overlooking his hometown of Cranbrook and was famously pictured hoisting it over his head.[[32]](#cite_note-SCMountain-32)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/S._Niedermayer_retirement.jpg/250px-S._Niedermayer_retirement.jpg)](/wiki/File:S._Niedermayer_retirement.jpg)

The Devils recognized Niedermayer's contributions to their franchise by retiring his uniform number 27 in 2011.

The expiration of his contract following the season resulted in another lengthy dispute with the Devils. While he wanted a contract similar to the $5.3 million per season average the top ten paid defencemen in the league made, the Devils offered a deal with a base salary of $3.5 million.[[33]](#cite_note-33) He was again a holdout at the start of the [2000–01 season](/wiki/2000%E2%80%9301_NHL_season "2000–01 NHL season"), and missed nearly two months of play before finally agreeing to a four-year, $16 million contract.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Niedermayer recorded 35 points in 57 games and played in his [second All-Star Game](/wiki/51st_National_Hockey_League_All-Star_Game "51st National Hockey League All-Star Game").[[12]](#cite_note-LOH-12) Late in game four of New Jersey's first round playoff series against the [Toronto Maple Leafs](/wiki/Toronto_Maple_Leafs "Toronto Maple Leafs"), Niedermayer was knocked unconscious by a vicious elbow from Toronto's [Tie Domi](/wiki/Tie_Domi "Tie Domi"). Niedermayer later claimed that Domi had threatened to retaliate against him over a previous hit earlier in the series.[[35]](#cite_note-35) Domi apologized for the incident, calling it a "stupid reaction", but was suspended for the remainder of the [2001 Stanley Cup playoffs](/wiki/2001_Stanley_Cup_playoffs "2001 Stanley Cup playoffs").[[36]](#cite_note-36) New Jersey reached the [2001 Stanley Cup Finals](/wiki/2001_Stanley_Cup_Finals "2001 Stanley Cup Finals"), but lost the series to the [Colorado Avalanche](/wiki/Colorado_Avalanche "Colorado Avalanche") in seven games.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

Niedermayer missed several games early in the [2001–02 season](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_NHL_season "2001–02 NHL season") due to back pain,[[38]](#cite_note-38) and his 33 points on the season was his lowest in six seasons.[[9]](#cite_note-NHLStats-9) Niedermayer and the Devils reached their fourth [Stanley Cup Finals](/wiki/2003_Stanley_Cup_Finals "2003 Stanley Cup Finals") in [2002–03](/wiki/2002%E2%80%9303_NHL_season "2002–03 NHL season"). The series was a family affair, as Scott's brother [Rob](/wiki/Rob_Niedermayer "Rob Niedermayer") was a member of the opposing [Mighty Ducks of Anaheim](/wiki/Mighty_Ducks_of_Anaheim "Mighty Ducks of Anaheim"). When asked, their mother admitted she was hoping Rob's Mighty Ducks would win as Scott already had two championships to his name.[[5]](#cite_note-McEvoy-5)[[39]](#cite_note-39) Scott dashed his brother and mother's hopes, recording two assists in the deciding seventh game to lead the Devils to a 3–0 victory, and win his third Stanley Cup.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Scott_Hannan_and_Scott_Niedermayer.jpg/250px-Scott_Hannan_and_Scott_Niedermayer.jpg)](/wiki/File:Scott_Hannan_and_Scott_Niedermayer.jpg)

Niedermayer battles for the puck with [Scott Hannan](/wiki/Scott_Hannan "Scott Hannan") of the [San Jose Sharks](/wiki/San_Jose_Sharks "San Jose Sharks") in his first season in Anaheim.

Niedermayer cemented his reputation as an elite NHL defenceman in [2003–04](/wiki/2003%E2%80%9304_NHL_season "2003–04 NHL season"), earning praise from both teammates and opponents for his play throughout the season.[[41]](#cite_note-NoticeNiedermayer-41) Injuries to fellow defencemen [Scott Stevens](/wiki/Scott_Stevens "Scott Stevens") and [Brian Rafalski](/wiki/Brian_Rafalski "Brian Rafalski") resulted in Niedermayer averaging over 25 minutes of ice time per game, sometimes topping 30, and he temporarily inherited the Devils' [captaincy](/wiki/Captain_(ice_hockey) "Captain (ice hockey)") from Stevens.[[41]](#cite_note-NoticeNiedermayer-41) Offensively, Niedermayer posted his second career 50-point season, finishing with 14 goals and 40 assists. He played in his [third](/wiki/54th_National_Hockey_League_All-Star_Game "54th National Hockey League All-Star Game") All-Star Game and was named a First Team All-Star for the first time.[[12]](#cite_note-LOH-12) After finishing second in league scoring amongst defencemen, recording a [plus-minus](/wiki/Plus%E2%80%93minus_(sports) "Plus–minus (sports)") rating of +20 and leading the Devils to a modern NHL record low 164 goals against, Niedermayer was voted the winner of the [James Norris Memorial Trophy](/wiki/James_Norris_Memorial_Trophy "James Norris Memorial Trophy") as the league's top defenceman.[[42]](#cite_note-LOHNorris0304-42)

Upon the expiration of his previous contract, Niedermayer was again a restricted [free agent](/wiki/Free_agent "Free agent"), and he again endured a difficult negotiation with the Devils, even after he changed [agents](/wiki/Sports_agent "Sports agent").[[43]](#cite_note-43) Initially demanding a five-year, $45 million contract, Niedermayer chose to go to [arbitration](/wiki/Arbitration "Arbitration"). He was awarded a one-year contract for the [2004–05 season](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_NHL_season "2004–05 NHL season"), and the $7 million salary he was given tied [John LeClair](/wiki/John_LeClair "John LeClair")'s award in 2000 as the highest ever given in arbitration.[[44]](#cite_note-44) However, the contract was wiped out when the entire season was cancelled as a result of the [2004–05 NHL lockout](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_NHL_lockout "2004–05 NHL lockout").[[45]](#cite_note-45)

### Anaheim Ducks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scott_Niedermayer&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Anaheim Ducks")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Snieds-ducks.jpg/250px-Snieds-ducks.jpg)](/wiki/File:Snieds-ducks.jpg)

Niedermayer with the Ducks in 2006.

As an unrestricted free agent in the summer of 2005, Niedermayer was in considerable demand; 14 teams contacted his agent on the first day they were allowed to talk to him.[[46]](#cite_note-46) The Devils offered him a five-year contract that would have paid him $7.8 million per season, the maximum allowed under the new [salary cap](/wiki/NHL_salary_cap "NHL salary cap"), but Niedermayer chose instead to sign a four-year contract worth $6.75 million per season with the Mighty Ducks of Anaheim.[[47]](#cite_note-47) He chose to take the shorter term and lower salary to play and try to win a Stanley Cup with his brother.[[48]](#cite_note-48) The Ducks immediately named Scott the team's captain.[[49]](#cite_note-49) In his first season in Anaheim, [2005–06](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_NHL_season "2005–06 NHL season"), Niedermayer scored 63 points and was named a First Team All-Star for the second time.[[12]](#cite_note-LOH-12) In the [playoffs](/wiki/2006_Stanley_Cup_playoffs "2006 Stanley Cup playoffs"), the Ducks reached the [Western Conference](/wiki/Western_Conference_(NHL) "Western Conference (NHL)") Final, but were eliminated by the [Edmonton Oilers](/wiki/Edmonton_Oilers "Edmonton Oilers").[[50]](#cite_note-50)

Joined on Anaheim's blueline by [Chris Pronger](/wiki/Chris_Pronger "Chris Pronger"), Niedermayer helped the Ducks set franchise records in [2006–07](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_NHL_season "2006–07 NHL season") for most wins (48) and [points](/wiki/Point_(ice_hockey) "Point (ice hockey)") (110).[[51]](#cite_note-51) Both were named finalists for the Norris Trophy, but lost to Detroit's [Nicklas Lidström](/wiki/Nicklas_Lidstr%C3%B6m "Nicklas Lidström").[[52]](#cite_note-52) Individually, Niedermayer played his [1,000th career game](/wiki/List_of_NHL_players_with_1000_games_played "List of NHL players with 1000 games played"), against the [Edmonton Oilers](/wiki/Edmonton_Oilers "Edmonton Oilers") on November 28, 2006.[[53]](#cite_note-53) He set career highs of 15 goals, 54 assists and 69 points during the regular season and was named a First Team All-Star.[[12]](#cite_note-LOH-12) He added 11 points in the [2007 Stanley Cup playoffs](/wiki/2007_Stanley_Cup_playoffs "2007 Stanley Cup playoffs"),[[9]](#cite_note-NHLStats-9) and was named recipient of the [Conn Smythe Memorial Trophy](/wiki/Conn_Smythe_Memorial_Trophy "Conn Smythe Memorial Trophy") as the most valuable player of the post-season after leading Anaheim to the franchise's first championship in a five-game series victory over the [Ottawa Senators](/wiki/Ottawa_Senators "Ottawa Senators") in the [final](/wiki/2007_Stanley_Cup_Finals "2007 Stanley Cup Finals").[[54]](#cite_note-54) As team captain, Niedermayer was the first player given the chance to hoist the Stanley Cup. He passed the trophy to his brother; it was Rob's first championship victory. Of the moment, Scott stated: "You don't really dream of passing it to your brother. I never have. To be able to do that is definitely a highlight of my career."[[55]](#cite_note-55)

Coming off his fourth championship, the 34-year-old Niedermayer contemplated retirement.[[56]](#cite_note-PossibleRetirement-56) Remaining undecided on his future as the [2007–08 season](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_NHL_season "2007–08 NHL season") began, he failed to report to the team and was suspended by the Ducks as a formality.[[57]](#cite_note-57) Pronger replaced him as captain,[[56]](#cite_note-PossibleRetirement-56) and Niedermayer remained undecided until early December when he finally chose to return.[[58]](#cite_note-58) He appeared in only 48 games that season, scoring 25 points, but played in his fourth All-Star Game.[[12]](#cite_note-LOH-12)

After the Ducks were eliminated in the first round of the [2008 playoffs](/wiki/2008_Stanley_Cup_playoffs "2008 Stanley Cup playoffs"), he again contemplated retirement, but quickly made the decision to return for the [2008–09 season](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_NHL_season "2008–09 NHL season").[[59]](#cite_note-59) He regained captaincy of the Ducks,[[60]](#cite_note-60) and played in his fifth All-Star Game during the season in which he scored 59 points in 82 games.[[12]](#cite_note-LOH-12) In what proved to be his final season, [2009–10](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_NHL_season "2009–10 NHL season"), Niedermayer scored 48 points in 80 games.[[9]](#cite_note-NHLStats-9) He announced his retirement as a player on June 22, 2010, but remained with the Ducks organization as a consultant to general manager [Bob Murray](/wiki/Bob_Murray_(ice_hockey_b._1954) "Bob Murray (ice hockey b. 1954)").[[61]](#cite_note-TSNRetirement-61) He turned to coaching in [2012–13 season](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_NHL_season "2012–13 NHL season"), serving as a special assignment coach with the Ducks.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

## International play

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scott_Niedermayer&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: International play")]

Niedermayer enjoyed a long and successful international career, winning championships at all levels.[[63]](#cite_note-CSHOF-63) He made his international debut as a 17-year-old with the [Canadian junior team](/wiki/Canada_men%27s_national_junior_ice_hockey_team "Canada men's national junior ice hockey team") at the [1991 World Junior Ice Hockey Championships](/wiki/1991_World_Junior_Ice_Hockey_Championships "1991 World Junior Ice Hockey Championships").[[64]](#cite_note-2012IIHFRB-64) He appeared in three games as Team Canada used a late goal by [John Slaney](/wiki/John_Slaney "John Slaney") in the final game of the tournament, against the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union_national_junior_ice_hockey_team "Soviet Union national junior ice hockey team"), to win the gold medal.[[65]](#cite_note-65) He returned for the [1992 tournament](/wiki/1992_World_Junior_Ice_Hockey_Championships "1992 World Junior Ice Hockey Championships"), one which [The Sports Network](/wiki/The_Sports_Network "The Sports Network") described as one of the most disappointing in Canadian history, as the team finished sixth in the eight team event.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

Four years later, in [1996](/wiki/1996_World_Cup_of_Hockey "1996 World Cup of Hockey"), Niedermayer first joined the [senior team](/wiki/Canada_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Canada men's national ice hockey team") at the inaugural [World Cup of Hockey](/wiki/World_Cup_of_Hockey "World Cup of Hockey").[[64]](#cite_note-2012IIHFRB-64) He had a goal and three assists in eight games,[[9]](#cite_note-NHLStats-9) but Canada lost the championship final to the [United States](/wiki/United_States_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "United States men's national ice hockey team").[[67]](#cite_note-67) Niedermayer next made his [Olympic](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") debut in [2002](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2002_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 2002 Winter Olympics"). He appeared in six games, and helped Canada defeat the United States, 5–2 in the final as the country won its first Olympic gold medal in hockey in 50 years.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

Gold medal victories followed in 2004 as Niedermayer scored five points in nine games at the [World Championship](/wiki/2004_Men%27s_World_Ice_Hockey_Championships "2004 Men's World Ice Hockey Championships") then added two points in six games at the [World Cup of Hockey](/wiki/2004_World_Cup_of_Hockey "2004 World Cup of Hockey").[[64]](#cite_note-2012IIHFRB-64) In the latter event, Niedermayer scored a goal in the championship game, a 3–2 victory over [Finland](/wiki/Finland_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Finland men's national ice hockey team").[[69]](#cite_note-69) He was set to make his second Olympic appearance in [2006](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2006_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 2006 Winter Olympics"), however a knee injury suffered during NHL play that required surgery forced him to withdraw from the tournament.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

Niedermayer's final international competition came at the [2010 Olympics](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2010_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 2010 Winter Olympics"), four months before his retirement as a player.[[61]](#cite_note-TSNRetirement-61) He was named captain of the team for the tournament that was held in his home province, in [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver").[[71]](#cite_note-71) He led Canada to the gold medal, culminating in a 3–2 overtime win over the United States.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

## Playing style

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scott_Niedermayer&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Playing style")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Scott_Niedermayer-2006awards.jpg/250px-Scott_Niedermayer-2006awards.jpg)](/wiki/File:Scott_Niedermayer-2006awards.jpg)

Niedermayer at the NHL Awards in 2006.

An offensively-minded defenceman, Niedermayer was best known for his skating ability and drew comparison's to the game's offensive greats from the time he broke into the NHL.[[73]](#cite_note-1997Profile-73) He was compared to [Paul Coffey](/wiki/Paul_Coffey "Paul Coffey") for his ability to take the puck from his own goal line and convert a defensive situation into an offensive rush.[[74]](#cite_note-74) He often chafed at playing within the Devils' defence-oriented system, feeling it restricted his offensive creativity, but also admitted that he learned to place greater emphasis on his defence and develop his overall game.[[73]](#cite_note-1997Profile-73) Niedermayer earned a reputation for inconsistency early in his career.[[28]](#cite_note-FeelSorry-28) He often played his best games against top opposition, but his failure to consistently apply his skills against all opposition occasionally frustrated his teammates and left his coaches lamenting that he was a player capable of being dominant but often was not.[[75]](#cite_note-75) By the end of his career however, and following his Norris Trophy win in 2004, Niedermayer was regarded as one of the top offensive defencemen of his generation and as one of the game's greatest winners.[[76]](#cite_note-76) Niedermayer and former Ducks teammate Corey Perry are the only players in hockey history to win every major contemporary North American and International Competitive titles: the Memorial Cup, World Junior championship, Stanley Cup, World Championship, World Cup and Olympic Games.[[63]](#cite_note-CSHOF-63)

Niedermayer has been honoured on several occasions. The New Jersey Devils [retired](/wiki/List_of_NHL_retired_numbers "List of NHL retired numbers") his jersey number 27 in 2011,[[77]](#cite_note-77) and the Kamloops Blazers retired the number 28 he wore in junior in 2013.[[78]](#cite_note-78) He has been inducted into the [BC Hockey Hall of Fame](/w/index.php?title=BC_Hockey_Hall_of_Fame&action=edit&redlink=1 "BC Hockey Hall of Fame (page does not exist)") and [Canada's Sports Hall of Fame](/wiki/Canada%27s_Sports_Hall_of_Fame "Canada's Sports Hall of Fame").[[63]](#cite_note-CSHOF-63)[[79]](#cite_note-79) Niedermayer was elected to the [Hockey Hall of Fame](/wiki/Hockey_Hall_of_Fame "Hockey Hall of Fame") as part of its 2013 class, and was inducted on November 11, 2013.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scott_Niedermayer&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Niedermayer and his wife Lisa have four sons.[[81]](#cite_note-RMHOrange-81) The family resides in [Penticton, British Columbia](/wiki/Penticton,_British_Columbia "Penticton, British Columbia"), but often returns to Cranbrook, where they spent the off-seasons while he was playing.[[82]](#cite_note-82) Scott and Lisa are active in the community. They serve as honorary co-chairs of the Walk for Kids, a charity event that supports the [Ronald McDonald House](/wiki/Ronald_McDonald_House_Charities "Ronald McDonald House Charities") of [Orange County](/wiki/Orange_County,_California "Orange County, California"),[[83]](#cite_note-83) while Scott and his brother Rob operate a hockey school in Cranbrook and established a fund that offers grants to community associations.[[84]](#cite_note-84) An active [environmentalist](/wiki/Environmentalism "Environmentalism"), Scott has also joined with [WWF-Canada](/wiki/WWF-Canada "WWF-Canada") to speak out in favour of efforts to maintain British Columbia's natural wilderness.[[85]](#cite_note-85)
